titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the L. Paul Bremer III Papers
titlestmt.author: compiled by Joshua D. Cochran

controlnote.p: This encoded finding aid is compliant with the Yale EAD Best Practice Guidelines, Version 1.0.

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Friday November 29, 2024 at 05:45

did.physdesc: (54 boxes)
did.abstract: The collection documents the career of L. Paul Bremer III in the United States State Department and his work in the private sector. The bulk of material in the collection centers on Bremer's tenure as administrator of the Coalition Provisional Authority in Iraq from May 2003 until June 2004. This material includes subject files, correspondence with his family, contemporaneous notes, speeches, and his daily schedules while in Iraq. Material related to the writing of his 2006 memoir, My Year in Iraq, is also part of the collection. Additional material including editorials, interviews, working papers, and notes provide insight into United States foreign relations during the final decade of the Cold War and the global war on terrorism.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: The collection is open for research.

Original born digital files, as well as preservation masters, may not be accessed due to their fragility. Researchers must consult use copies, or if none exist request that they be made. Born digital files cannot be accessed remotely. System requirements include a Manuscripts and Archives computer and file viewing software.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Received from L. Paul Bremer III, 2021.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged into four series: I. Government service, 1976-2002; II. Professional and personal activities, 1989-2003; III. Administrator, Coalition Provisional Authority in Iraq, 2003-2004; IV: Writings and activities, 2004-2011.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Lewis Paul "Jerry" Bremer III was born on September 30, 1941, in Hartford, Connecticut to Lewis Bremer, Jr., president of Christian Dior perfumes, and Nina Bremer, teacher of art and architectural history at the University of Bridgeport. Bremer graduated from Yale College in 1963. He received a certificate in political studies from the Institut d'Etudes Politiques de Paris in 1964 and an MBA from Harvard Business School in 1966. Bremer married Frances Winfield on June 11, 1966.

Bremer joined the United States Foreign Service serving at posts in Kabul, Afghanistan and Malawi during the late 1960s and early 1970s. In 1973, Bremer returned to the United States as executive secretary for United States Secretary of State Henry Kissinger until 1976. He worked as deputy chief of mission at the United States Embassy in Oslo, Norway from 1976 until 1979. In the early 1980s, he became special assistant to the United States secretary of state. In 1983, United State president Ronald Reagan nominated Bremer to be ambassador to the Netherlands. In 1986, Reagan nominated Bremer to be United States ambassador-at-large for counter terrorism. Bremer retired from the United States State Department in 1989.

Bremer worked in the private sector as managing director of Kissinger Associates and later as chief executive officer and chairman of Marsh Crisis Consulting. Bremer performed consulting work and spoke to civic and trade groups about the post-Cold War climate for international business and how corporations might manage their overseas risk. Bremer returned to government service in 1999 when tapped to chair the National Commission on Terrorism from 1999 until 2000. Informally known as the "Bremer Commission," the bipartisan committee issued its report and recommendations, "Countering the Changing Threat of International Terrorism," in June 2000. Subsequently, Bremer served on the United States Congressional Advisory Panel to Assess Domestic Response Capabilities for Terrorism Involving Weapons of Mass Destruction, known as the Gilmore Commission, from 2000 until 2002. During this time, and in the aftermath of the September 11, 2001 terrorist attacks, Bremer published numerous op-ed pieces and gave interviews discussing terrorism and counter terrorism policy.

In March 2003, the United States invaded Iraq, citing the need to conduct a preemptive military strike against Saddam Hussein's regime due to allegations Iraq harbored weapons of mass destruction and international terrorists, as well as the Bush administration's desire to promote democracy in the Middle East. Although the United States toppled the Hussein regime and occupied Baghdad by April, the situation quickly deteriorated during the United States occupation due to a humanitarian crisis, economic insecurity, political instability and violence caused by rival factions seeking primacy in the post-Saddam Hussein era, and competing visions within the United States government on how best to rebuild and stabilize Iraq.

On May 9, 2003, President George W. Bush nominated Bremer to serve as United States presidential envoy to Iraq, reporting to Secretary of Defense Donald Rumsfeld. Bremer assumed responsibility for the civilian administration in Iraq, heading the newly formed Coalition Provisional Authority (CPA), which replaced the United State's Office for Reconstruction and Humanitarian Assistance (ORHA). Bremer was tasked with stabilizing Iraq and creating conditions that could permit the country's transition to a democratic state under the control of the Iraqi people. He helped establish the Iraqi Governing Council consisting of Iraqi leaders of different religious and ethnic groups who were charged with drafting a constitution and constructing a framework for government. Bremer worked with the Iraqi Governing Council and national leaders, the United States and coalition political establishment, military officials, contractors, and international organizations to confront a host of issues in the country. Among the most pressing challenges for Bremer and the CPA included the removing Ba'athist influence, reconstructing the disbanded Iraqi army, fostering civil society, addressing a growing insurgency directed against United States and coalition forces, restoring basic public services, and responding to torture and human rights abuses by United States soldiers at the Abu Ghraib Prison. As CPA administrator, Bremer announced the capture of Saddam Hussein by United States forces on December 14, 2003. On behalf of the United States government and coalitions forces, Bremer formally transferred governing authority to the Iraq Interim Government on June 28, 2004.

Bremer has continued to write and speak on his experiences in Iraq and in the United States foreign service. In December 2004, President George W. Bush awarded Bremer the Presidential Medal of Freedom. In 2006, Bremer published a memoir on his time in Iraq, My Year in Iraq: the Struggle to Build a Future of Hope, co-authored with Malcolm McConnell.

chronlist.head: Career Outline of L. Paul Bremer, III

chronitemset.event: Certificate of Political Studies, Institut d'Etudes Politiques de Paris
Executive Secretary to United States Secretary of State Henry Kissinger
United States Deputy Chief of Mission, Norway
Special Assistant and Executive Secretary to United States Secretary of State
United States Ambassador to the Netherlands
United States Ambassador-at-Large for Counter Terrorism
Chairman, National Commission on Terrorism (Bremer Commission)
Member, United States Congressional Advisory Panel to Assess Domestic Response Capabilities for Terrorism Involving Weapons of Mass Destruction (Gilmore Commission)
Chairman and Chief Executive Officer, Marsh Crisis Consulting
United States Presidential Envoy to Iraq and Administrator of Coalition Provisional Authority
Awarded United States Presidenital Medal of Freedom by George W. Bush
Published, My Year in Iraq: The Struggle to Build a Future of Hope , co-authored with Malcolm McConnell

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: L. Paul Bremer III Papers (MS 2123). Manuscripts and Archives, Yale University Library.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The papers document Bremer's career in government service and the private sector from 1976 through 2011. The collection highlights Bremer's service as the United States presidential envoy and administrator of the Coalition Provisional Authority in Iraq from May 2003 until June 2004. Bremer's Iraq material in Series III includes his contemporaneous notes, working files, speeches, daily schedules, and personal correspondence with his family. Documents and photographs showcase his work, daily life, and relationships with officials from the United States, Iraq, and other countries and international organizations who were involved in the country's rebuilding following the regime of Saddam Hussein and the United State military invasion. While in Iraq, Bremer corresponded with his wife Frances daily. His outgoing letters track his daily activities, meetings, travels, and conversations, providing a detailed account of his time in Iraq.

Series III also include the Coalition Provisional Authority executive secretary archive. This archive was maintained by Coalition Provisional Authority Executive Secretary Jessica LeCroy, who reported directly to Bremer. This executive secretary archive includes reports, presentations, briefing papers, budgets, surveys, strategic plans, subject files, legal orders, memoranda. The material was housed on fifteen disks, and Bremer later provided the disks to the Rand Corporation for a study they conducted following the United States occupation. The Rand Corporation returned the disks and twenty-seven binders with materials printed from the disks. Both the fifteen born digital disks and the printed material from the twenty-seven binders are housed in this series. Additional materials on Bremer's time in Iraq are found in Series IV, including items from the preparation of his memoir My Time in Iraq: The Struggle to Build a Future of Hope published in 2006.

Speeches from Bremer's tenure as United States deputy chief of mission to Norway (1976 to 1979) and ambassador to the Netherlands (1983 to 1986) focus on United States foreign relations with NATO allies during the final decade of the Cold War. The speeches address a range of issues, including arms control, international trade and commerce, perspectives on United States-Soviet relations, and cultural diplomacy.

Speeches, editorials, interviews, and working files from Bremer's service as United States Ambassador-at-Large for Counterterrorism (1986-1989) and as Chairman of the National Commission on Terrorism, known as the Bremer Commission (1999-2000), highlight the concerns and debates over global terrorism and counterterrorism in the 1980s and 1990s.

The collection also has press clippings, interviews, and speeches from Bremer's private sector work as chief executive officer of Marsh Crisis Consulting and managing director of Kissinger Associates during the 1990s. These materials detail Bremer's assessment of the global business climate in the aftermath of the Cold War and how corporations might manage risk during at the time.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: Copyright is retained by L. Paul Bremer III for works authored or otherwise produced by the donor. Copyright status for other collection materials is unknown. Transmission or reproduction of materials protected by U.S. Copyright Law (Title 17, U.S.C.) beyond that allowed by fair use requires the written permission of the copyright owners. Works not in the public domain cannot be commercially exploited without permission of the copyright owners. Responsibility for any use rests exclusively with the user.

subject.part: Foreign policy of the United States

corpname.part: Advisory Panel to Assess Domestic Response Capabilities for Terrorism Involving Weapons of Mass Destruction (U.S.)
International Advisory and Monitoring Board for Iraq
United States. Agency for International Development
United States. National Commission on Terrorism
United States. Office of the Special Inspector General for Iraq Reconstruction

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This series is arranged chronologically into six groupings: United States deputy chief of mission to Norway, 1976-1979; United States ambassador to the Netherlands, 1983-1986; United States ambassador-at-large for counter terrorism, 1986-1989; chairman, Department of State study group for Generalist Personnel System, 1989; Chairman, National Commission on Terrorism, 1999-2000; and member, United States Congressional Advisory Panel to assess domestic response capabilities for terrorism involving weapons of mass destruction, 2000-2002.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Speech and speech related material from Bremer's time deputy chief of mission at the United States Embassy in Oslo, Norway from 1976 to 1979 and as United States ambassador to the Netherlands from 1983 to 1986.

Other material includes files, editorials, interviews, reports and congressional testimony document Bremer's service as United States ambassador-at-large for counter terrorism from 1986 to 1989 and as chairman on the National Commission on Terrorism in 1999-2000.

did.unittitle: United States deputy chief of mission to Norway

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Speech and speech related material (e.g., notes, talking points) from Bremer's service as deputy chief of mission to Norway. Titles have been repurposed from the folders provided by Ambassador Bremer.

did.unittitle: Notes for speech on major aspects of U.S. foreign policy today
Fourth of July speech at Lincoln Memorial in Oslo
New conventional weapons technology and East-West security
American foreign policy under President Carter
Three hundred years of American architecture, from 1660 to 1960
United States ambassador to the Netherlands

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Speech and speech related material (e.g., notes, talking points) from Bremer's service as United States ambassador to the Netherlands. Titles have been repurposed from the folders provided by Ambassador Bremer.

Toward a framework for real mutual restraint

Dealing with piracy and hostage-taking by the Barbary powers, 1780-1819

Address to special agent class XXXV
Conference on Middle East fundamentalism and terrorism
Essential ingredients in the fight against terrorism
Countering terrorism: United States policy in the 1980s and 1990s
Terrorism: its evolving nature (State Department Bulletin)
Chairman, Department of State study group for generalist personnel system
Chairman, National Commission on Terrorism (Bremer Commission)
Briefing book, National Commission on Terrorism

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This material is unprocessed and may contain sensitive information or be in a physical state that would prohibit use. Researchers wishing to request access should email beinecke.library@yale.edu. The request should outline the scope and purpose of the research project, why the researcher believes the material is relevant to their project, and contact information. The review may take several weeks.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Original born digital files, as well as preservation masters, may not be accessed due to their fragility. Researchers must consult use copies, or if none exist request that they be made. Born digital files cannot be accessed remotely. System requirements include a Manuscripts and Archives computer and file viewing software.

did.unittitle: Report from the National Commission on Terrorism
Member, United States Congressional Advisory Panel to Assess Domestic Response Capabilities for Terrorism Involving Weapons of Mass Destruction (Gilmore Commission)
Defending the American Homeland (Heritage Foundation report with Ed Meese)
Patterns of Global Terrorism (United States State Department)

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This series is arranged into three groupings: Articles, interviews, and speeches 1989-2003; Computer files, 2000-2003; and Press clippings, 2001-2002. Materials are arranged chronologically within each group.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series includes Bremer's speeches, articles, and interviews while serving as managing director at Kissinger Associates from 1989 to 2000 and as chief executive officer and chairman of Marsh Crisis Consulting Practices. Materials showcase Bremer's assessment of the global business climate and how to manage risk in the aftermath of the Cold War.

did.unittitle: Prospects for EC92 (Netherlands Board of Tourism)
Talking notes on United States foreign policy
Intelligence strategies columns for CEO Magazine
Speech to the National Peanut Council (EC92)
United States role in Europe and the world in the 1990s
New world disorder--what it means for the United States
Situation in the Union of Soviet Socialist Republics (USSR)
Prospects for peaceful reform in the former Soviet Union
Iran and Syria--keep the bums out ( New York Times editorial)
Economic Development and political stability (Dr. Kissinger)
Is there a new world order?
America and Russia in the new world order
Testimony for House Foreign Affairs Committee
China's place in the international order
Talking points for Johnson and Johnson
With Assad, talk about terrorism ( Wall Street Journal editorial)
Business opportunities and challenges in the post-Cold War world
Trade and investment trends to the end of the century
Seizing the inititative: the U.S. role in combatting global terrorism
Terrorist challenges after the Cold War
Geopolitical risks in the post Cold War world
Senator Hank Brown hearing on terrorism
Terrorists' patrons must pay a price ( Wall Street Journal editorial)
Counterterrorism: the need for international cooperation
International responsibility in the post-Cold War world
How to tackle the new terrorists ( New York Daily News Editorial)
Statement on counter terrorism (United States House Committee on International Relations)
The shadows of history: China in the 21st century
Defining America's role in global security
New terrorist threats and how to counter them (Heritage Foundation)
Notes for Philadelphia World Affairs Council
Marsh hires expert on counterterrorism (National Underwriter announcement)
Interview with Business for Breakfast (KKZN ABC Radio, Denver)
Lessons from the East Africa bombing trials
Presentation to U.S. House Intelligence Committee
Let us wage total war on our foes ( Wall Street Journal l)
Interview with Robert J. Caldwell of San Diego Union Tribune
The need for a new counter-terrorist strategy
A new strategy for the new face of terror
National security for a new era: focusing national power
Politieke risico's in emerging markets nemen toe (Political risks in emerging markets are increasing)
Interview with Joe Harrington of American Association of Insurance Services
Interview with Business for Breakfast (KKZN ABC Radio, Denver)
The war on terrorism: challenges for U.S.-European relations
Relations franco-américains: que s'est-ill passé (French-American relations: what happened?)

did.unittitle: Administrator, Coalition Provisional Authority in Iraq

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This series is arranged into three groupings: Bremer files, 2003-2004; Coalition Provisional Authority files, 2003-2004 and Photographs, 2003-2004. Materials within these groupings are arranged alphabetically into subgroupings.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series include Bremer's contemporaneous notes, working files, speeches, daily schedules, and personal correspondence with his family while serving as the United States presidential envoy and administrator of the Coalition Provisional Authority in Iraq from May 2003 until June 2004. The series also includes several digital photographs taken by military and civilian officials that document Bremer and the Coalition Provisional Authority's work in Iraq.

Bremer's daily correspondence with his wife, Frances, discusses his activities, travels, and work in Iraq. In the correspondence, Bremer details meetings and conversations with, and impressions of, individuals involved with the United States occupation and rebuilding effort in Iraq. Frequent subjects in Bremer's correspondence include United States Secretary of Defense Donald Rumsfeld, Rumsfeld's deputy Paul Wolfowitz, undersecretary of defense Douglas Feith, United States Secretary of State Colin Powell, United States National Security Advisor Condoleezza Rice, Commander of United States Central Command John Abizaid, Commander of Coalition Ground Forces in Iraq Ricardo B. Sánchez, and Coalition Provisional Authority officials Dan Senor and Peter McPherson. Bremer's letters mention his meetings and discussions with and about prominent Iraqis including presidents of the Iraq Governing Council Iyad Allawi, Massoud Barzini, Ahmed Chalabi, and Jalel Talabani; Iraqi politician Adnan Pachachi; prominent Ayatollahs Mohammad Bahr al-Ulloum and Ali al-Husayni Sistani; and cleric Muqtada Sadr.

Series III also includes the Coalition Provisional Authority Executive Secretary Archive. This archive was maintained by Coalition Provisional Authority Executive Secretary Jessica LeCroy, who reported directly to Bremer. This executive secretary archive includes reports, presentations, briefing papers, budgets, surveys, strategic plans, subject files, legal orders, memoranda. The material was housed on fifteen computer disks, and Bremer later provided the disks to the Rand Corporation for a study they conducted following the United States occupation. The Rand Corporation returned the disks and twenty-seven binders with materials printed from the disk. Both the fifteen computer disks and the printed material from the twenty-seven binders are housed in this series.

The review may take several weeks.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: This material was imaged or captured by Beinecke staff in 2024 for preservation and access. Additional information about actions taken may be available at the repository.

did.unittitle: Department of Defense, Office of General Counsel, Authorities
Office of General Counsel for LPB (computer files)

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This material is unprocessed and may contain sensitive information or be in a physical state that would prohibit use. Researchers wishing to request access should email beinecke.library@yale.edu. The request should outline the scope and purpose of the research project, why the researcher believes the material is relevant to their project, and contact information. The review may take several weeks.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Original born digital files, as well as preservation masters, may not be accessed due to their fragility. Researchers must consult use copies, or if none exist request that they be made. Born digital files cannot be accessed remotely. System requirements include a Manuscripts and Archives computer and file viewing software.

processinfo.p: This material was imaged or captured by Beinecke staff in 2024 for preservation and access. Additional information about actions taken may be available at the repository.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Two handwritten notes from President George W. Bush. The first is from November 30, 2003 regarding President Bush's surprise visit to Iraq for Thanksgiving. The second is from November 10, 2006 thanking Bremer and his wife for their recent note.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Note on front cover reads, "LPB, Here are just a few remembered moments...but I have many more. I hope you enjoy them. This one is of you coming home in a Blackhawk taken from my Blackhawk (5-17-04). Christina Estrada

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This series is arranged in two groupings: My Year in Iraq, 2003-2006 and Post-Iraq reports, studies, and lessons, 2004-2011. These groupings are futher arranged into subgroups, which are listed alphabetically.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Drafts, correspondence, book reviews, and photographs related to the preparation and publication of Bremer's memoir, My Year in Iraq: The Struggle to Build a Future of Hope co-written with Malcolm McConnell and published in 2006. This series also includes Bremer's other writings and studies on Iraq, as well as government reports and studies of the United States occupation of Iraq.

title.part: My Year in Iraq: The Struggle to Build a Future of Hope

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This grouping contains notes, emails, and other materials Bremer compiled during the writing of his memoir. The material is especially rich in printed email exchanges Bremer had with colleagues about their time, impressions, and memories of Iraq.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Some folders contain material for chapters immediately preceding it or following it, accounting for Ambassador Bremer and his editor's writing and editing process.

did.unittitle: Book draft with comments by Bremer, Malcolm McConnell, and editors

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This material is unprocessed and may contain sensitive information or be in a physical state that would prohibit use. Researchers wishing to request access should email beinecke.library@yale.edu. The request should outline the scope and purpose of the research project, why the researcher believes the material is relevant to their project, and contact information. The review may take several weeks.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Original born digital files, as well as preservation masters, may not be accessed due to their fragility. Researchers must consult use copies, or if none exist request that they be made. Born digital files cannot be accessed remotely. System requirements include a Manuscripts and Archives computer and file viewing software.
